quote: The fluid begins to run with the first mild weather in March.
citation: Cooper, Susan Fenimore. Rural Hours. 3rd ed. New York: George P. Putnam, 1850.

quote: the dark spathe of the skunk-cabbage makes its way in the midst of snow and ice.
citation: Cooper, Susan Fenimore. Rural Hours. 3rd ed. New York: George P. Putnam, 1850.

quote: It is singular that at a moment when the soil generally is frost-bound, any plant should find out that spring is at hand; but toward the close of February, or beginning of March, the skunk-cabbage makes a good guess at the time of the year.
citation: Cooper, Susan Fenimore. Rural Hours. 3rd ed. New York: George P. Putnam, 1850.

quote: Although a very common plant, many persons familiar with its broad glossy leaves in summer, have never seen the flower, and have no idea how early it blossoms.
citation: Cooper, Susan Fenimore. Rural Hours. 3rd ed. New York: George P. Putnam, 1850.

quote: In the neighborhood of the village, it has been up these three weeks.
citation: Cooper, Susan Fenimore. Rural Hours. 3rd ed. New York: George P. Putnam, 1850.

quote: The blue hyacinths soon follow, though they will scarcely bloom yet this fortnight.
citation: Cooper, Susan Fenimore. Rural Hours. 3rd ed. New York: George P. Putnam, 1850.

quote: The garden hyacinths, and daffodils, and common lilies are beginning to show their leaves in the flower-borders.
citation: Cooper, Susan Fenimore. Rural Hours. 3rd ed. New York: George P. Putnam, 1850.

quote: A large amount of this sugar is still made in our neighborhood, chiefly for home consumption on the farms.
citation: Cooper, Susan Fenimore. Rural Hours. 3rd ed. New York: George P. Putnam, 1850.

quote: Some years it continues to flow at intervals until the last of April.
citation: Cooper, Susan Fenimore. Rural Hours. 3rd ed. New York: George P. Putnam, 1850.

quote: the periwinkle is almost in blossom : this is one of the very earliest flowers to open with us.
citation: Cooper, Susan Fenimore. Rural Hours. 3rd ed. New York: George P. Putnam, 1850.

quote: The snow-drop seldom opens here before the middle or third week of April, remaining in flower until the tulips fade, early in June.
citation: Cooper, Susan Fenimore. Rural Hours. 3rd ed. New York: George P. Putnam, 1850.

quote: maples that have been tapped for fifty years or more, are just as luxuriant in their foliage and flowers, as those that are untouched.
citation: Cooper, Susan Fenimore. Rural Hours. 3rd ed. New York: George P. Putnam, 1850.

quote: Bright sun, but cool air, which keeps back the swelling buds; and if these come out too soon they are in great danger of being injured by frost.
citation: Cooper, Susan Fenimore. Rural Hours. 3rd ed. New York: George P. Putnam, 1850.

quote: Berries were very plenty; it was a perfect bed of the squaw-vine and partridge berry.
citation: Cooper, Susan Fenimore. Rural Hours. 3rd ed. New York: George P. Putnam, 1850.

quote: Like the orange-tree, this humble plant bears fruit and flowers together; its white cups hanging side by side with the coral berries through the mild weather, from early in May to the sharpest frosts in October.
citation: Cooper, Susan Fenimore. Rural Hours. 3rd ed. New York: George P. Putnam, 1850.

quote: It is in June that the berries break open and drop the seed.
citation: Cooper, Susan Fenimore. Rural Hours. 3rd ed. New York: George P. Putnam, 1850.

quote: Berries were very plenty; it was a perfect bed of the squaw-vine and partridge berry.
citation: Cooper, Susan Fenimore. Rural Hours. 3rd ed. New York: George P. Putnam, 1850.

quote: The flowers are slender delicate pink bells, pale without, deep rose-color within; they are very fragrant.
citation: Cooper, Susan Fenimore. Rural Hours. 3rd ed. New York: George P. Putnam, 1850.
